title: Outdoor Daybed with Canopy Posts
summary: A raised twin-size daybed on four 80"-tall corner posts — the posts double as canopy supports, with a slatted mattress deck and a trimmed-out privacy back.
description:
Most outdoor daybeds sit low, cushion-first. This one stands the whole structure up on four 80"-tall 2x4 legs, which turns the frame into its own canopy support — string a curtain rod, sail shade, or drape between the four posts and the daybed becomes a shaded outdoor nook without a separate canopy frame to engineer. The posts are simply cut long from the start; there's no added bracket or extension involved.

Build the two identical side panels first, pocket-holed at every horizontal-to-leg joint, then the front and back panels the same way — the 2x6 boards get an extra pocket hole per end over the 2x4s, since they're carrying more of the structure's own weight. Keep every pocket hole on the inside faces as you go; nothing about this build shows a fastener from outside once it's finished (pocket holes can also simply be filled with plugs or exterior wood filler if one works loose from view).

The back gets a full privacy treatment: 2x2 trim flush to the back edge, slatted boards across it, then a capping course of 2x2 and 2x4 that finishes the top edge cleanly. It's the one side of this daybed that reads as a real backrest instead of open framing.

Like most slatted daybeds and platform beds, the mattress deck is sized around the space you actually built rather than a fixed number — the cut list gives 39" as a starting point, but the instruction is explicit: measure the opening between the 2x6 aprons and cut every slat to fit that real measurement, using up whatever 2x4, 2x2, and 2x6 offcuts are left over from the rest of the build.

tools.note: The mattress slats are measured and cut to fit the real opening, not a fixed length.

materials.name: 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Legs, front/back frame, and cleats — with enough leftover for mattress slats.

materials.name: Exterior wood filler or plugs
materials.note: Fills any pocket hole that ends up visible.

cutList.note: Tall on purpose — these double as canopy posts.
Starting estimate — measure the real opening between the aprons and cut every slat to fit.

steps.title: Build the two side panels
steps.body:
Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes on both ends of each horizontal board. Attach to the legs with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. Build two identical side panels.

Pocket holes can be filled with plugs or exterior wood filler if any end up visible in the finished piece.

steps.title: Build the front and back panels
steps.body: Drill two pocket holes on each end of every horizontal board — three per end on the 2x6 apron boards, which carry more of the structure's weight than the 2x4s. Attach to the legs with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, keeping every pocket hole on the inside faces where they stay hidden.

steps.title: Add the back trim
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 back trim pieces to the back side of the daybed with 2-1/2" self-tapping exterior screws, flush to the back edge.

steps.title: Slat the back and cap it
steps.body:
Attach the back slats to the 2x2 trim with 2-1/2" screws — you can also drive them from outside the leg for extra hold.

Complete the back by capping it with another course of 2x2 and a 2x4 cap, using the 2-1/2" screws throughout. This finishes the back into a real privacy panel rather than open framing.

steps.title: Install the cleats
steps.body: Attach the 2x4 cleats inside the 2x6 front and back aprons with 2-1/2" screws, spaced roughly every 8".

steps.title: Cut and lay the mattress slats
steps.body:
Measure the actual opening between your two 2x6 aprons — treat 39" as a starting estimate, not a fixed number. Cut the slats to fit from your 2x4, 2x2, and 2x6 offcuts, lay them on the cleats, and screw down with 2-1/2" screws.

Sand and finish the whole daybed. String a curtain rod, sail shade, or drape between the four tall posts for the canopy.

images.alt: Raised outdoor daybed on tall corner posts with a slatted privacy back
